encounter.reason: Outside-clinician triage call: increasing dyspnea, weight gain, recently d/c Lasix

conditions.name: Recurrent pleural effusion (h/o thoracenteses, patient describes prior procedures as painful)

medications.name: Furosemide (Lasix) 40 mg PO daily
medications.statusReason: Discontinued by cardiology 3/1/26 — see cardiology note

medications.name: Metoprolol succinate ER 25 mg PO daily

recentNotes.author: Cardiology, Synthetic MD
recentNotes.description: Cardiology follow-up: reviewed volume status, BP trending low, decision to discontinue Lasix and trial off diuretic. Patient counseled to monitor daily weights and report >2 lb gain over 48h. Recheck in 4 weeks. Prior thoracenteses tolerated poorly per patient ('disaster and painful'); avoid unless clearly indicated.

recentNotes.author: Pulmonology, Synthetic MD
recentNotes.description: Therapeutic right-sided thoracentesis. 1100 mL serous fluid drained. Procedure prolonged due to patient discomfort despite local anesthesia and PRN IV analgesia; patient repeatedly requested termination. Tolerated procedure poorly. Plan: avoid repeat unless symptom-driven and after explicit reconsent.

questions.rationale: Caller is VA RN initiating outside-clinician triage; need to establish what assessment has already been performed before duplicating effort.
questions.question: Renee, what's prompted the call today and what have you already assessed at your end (vitals, exam, SpO2)?

questions.rationale: Need to confirm setting and resources available to the VA RN to calibrate disposition options.
questions.question: Are you with the patient in person right now, or is this a phone check-in from the VA side?

questions.rationale: Cardiology 3/1/26 note cited 'BP trending low' as rationale for stopping Lasix; current BP is decision-relevant.
questions.question: What's the current BP and HR, and do you have a recent trend?
questions.expectedRange: SBP/DBP mmHg, HR bpm, with trend

questions.rationale: COPD plus CHF differential — SpO2 helps separate hypoxic decompensation from euvolemic dyspnea.
questions.question: Room-air SpO2, and is the patient on home O2 at baseline?
questions.expectedRange: % on room air or L/min if on O2

questions.rationale: Respiratory rate and work of breathing distinguish stable from impending respiratory failure in this CHF/COPD/AS patient.
questions.question: What's the respiratory rate, and is there accessory muscle use, tripoding, or audible wheeze?

questions.rationale: Afebrile vs febrile changes the differential toward pneumonia/COPD exacerbation rather than pure volume overload.
questions.question: Current temperature?

questions.rationale: Chart shows weights 188 → 188.7 → 189.5 lb over 2 weeks — only ~1.5 lb gain, below the >2 lb/48h threshold cardiology set on 3/1/26. Need to verify scale consistency before treating trend as real.
questions.question: Are these weights from the same scale, same time of day, similar clothing? Any home weights between the documented ones?

questions.rationale: Reason for call cites 'weight gain' but documented gain is modest; need patient's own dry-weight baseline since Lasix was stopped 3/1/26.
questions.question: What was the patient's weight around the time Lasix was discontinued in early March, and what does he consider his dry weight?

questions.rationale: Peripheral edema is a key bedside finding for post-diuretic-discontinuation volume reaccumulation.
questions.question: Any new lower-extremity edema — how high does it track, and is it new or worse than baseline?

questions.rationale: JVD is the most specific bedside sign of elevated right-sided filling pressures; relevant given CHF with stopped diuretic.
questions.question: Were you able to assess JVD, and if so what did you see?

questions.rationale: Ascites or abdominal fullness suggests right-heart congestion; cheap to ask peer-to-peer.
questions.question: Any abdominal distension, early satiety, or RUQ discomfort suggesting hepatic congestion?

questions.rationale: Dyspnea is the chief complaint; need timeline to distinguish gradual reaccumulation (post-Lasix d/c) from acute event (PE, MI, COPD exacerbation).
questions.question: Onset and tempo of the dyspnea — gradual over weeks since Lasix was stopped, or a more acute change in the last few days?

questions.rationale: Orthopnea and PND are more specific for cardiac/volume-driven dyspnea than COPD.
questions.question: Any orthopnea (extra pillows, sleeping upright) or PND? Change from baseline?

questions.rationale: History of recurrent pleural effusion with documented thoracentesis 9/4/24; reaccumulation is plausible and clinically important given prior poor tolerance.
questions.question: On exam, are breath sounds decreased or dull at the bases — particularly the right side where the prior effusion was tapped?

questions.rationale: COPD on the problem list — exacerbation can mimic CHF decompensation and is treated very differently.
questions.question: Any change in cough character, sputum volume or color, or recent URI?

questions.rationale: Wheeze vs crackles helps separate COPD exacerbation from pulmonary edema.
questions.question: On auscultation: wheeze, crackles, or both — and distribution?

questions.rationale: 76 yo male, CHF, AS, on aspirin and statin — ischemia is a competing cause of new dyspnea and would change disposition entirely.
questions.question: Any chest pain, pressure, jaw/arm discomfort, or anginal-equivalent symptoms with exertion?

questions.rationale: Aortic stenosis (active dx 2023) raises risk of exertional syncope/presyncope; symptom progression has prognostic and procedural implications.
questions.question: Any syncope, near-syncope, lightheadedness on exertion, or new exertional chest tightness?

questions.rationale: On metoprolol; new palpitations or HR changes after diuretic withdrawal could indicate AF or rate issues contributing to decompensation.
questions.question: Any palpitations, irregular pulse, or HR outside his usual range?

questions.rationale: Functional decline quantifies the dyspnea complaint and helps stratify urgency.
questions.question: Compared to a month ago, how far can he walk or what activities can he do before stopping for breath?

questions.rationale: Lasix discontinued 3/1/26 — central to this presentation. Confirm patient actually stopped and didn't restart on his own, and clarify what he was told about restart criteria.
questions.question: Has he taken any furosemide since 3/1/26, and does he have leftover supply at home?

questions.rationale: Lisinopril + low baseline BP (per 3/1 note) + sodium 131 raises concern for hypotension or worsening hyponatremia if volume status shifts.
questions.question: Any new dizziness, orthostasis, or symptomatic hypotension since the Lasix was stopped?

questions.rationale: Med adherence is a common decompensation driver; verify the active med list against what he's actually taking.
questions.question: Can you reconcile his current meds — specifically lisinopril 10, metoprolol succ 25, atorvastatin 40, ASA 81 — any missed doses or new meds (including OTC NSAIDs)?

questions.rationale: NSAIDs and high-sodium intake are classic precipitants of CHF decompensation, especially after stopping a diuretic.
questions.question: Any recent NSAID use, steroid bursts, or notable change in dietary sodium/fluid intake?

questions.rationale: Most recent NT-proBNP (3420, 2/15/26) and Na 131 are >2 months old and predate the Lasix discontinuation; they're stale for current decision-making.
questions.question: Does the VA have any labs since 3/1 — BMP, BNP/NT-proBNP, Cr — or can you draw today?

questions.rationale: Baseline Na 131 (low) on 2/15/26 — clinically meaningful for this presentation; symptoms of hyponatremia would shift acuity.
questions.question: Any confusion, headache, nausea, or new cognitive change that might suggest worsening hyponatremia?

questions.rationale: 9/4/24 thoracentesis tolerated poorly ('disaster and painful' per patient); 3/1 cardiology note explicitly says avoid unless clearly indicated and after reconsent. This must be flagged before any disposition that could lead to repeat tap.
questions.question: Has anyone discussed a possible repeat thoracentesis with the patient, and what's his current stance? Please flag the prior poor tolerance prominently in any referral.

questions.rationale: Standard CHF/respiratory red flags that would push toward ED rather than clinic.
questions.question: Any of the following: SpO2 <90% on baseline O2, RR >24, SBP <90, chest pain, hemoptysis, altered mental status, or inability to speak full sentences?

questions.rationale: Unilateral leg swelling/pain in a 76 yo with new dyspnea and reduced mobility raises PE concern, separate from CHF.
questions.question: Any unilateral calf swelling, pain, or recent immobility/travel that would raise DVT/PE concern?

questions.rationale: Knowing patient's preferences and prior negative experience with procedures shapes whether we route to clinic, ED, or home titration.
questions.question: What's the patient's preference and goals — clinic visit for restart of diuretic and labs, or is he requesting/willing to go to ED?

questions.rationale: Caregiver/transport availability affects feasibility of outpatient management for a 76 yo.
questions.question: Who's at home with him, and is transportation to clinic feasible today or tomorrow?

questions.rationale: Clean peer-to-peer closeout: confirm callback path and ensure VA RN has what she needs.
questions.question: Best callback number and your shift end — and is there anything else from your assessment we haven't covered?
